title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.
short_summary: A reflection on goodness, moral disgust, and the need to widen the mind enough to meet suffering without turning to hatred.

chapters.title: Opening the Heart
chapters.description: Matthew introduces kama muta and moral elevation as ways the heart is moved by goodness.

chapters.title: Wesley Autry's Courage
chapters.description: He tells the subway rescue story and uses it to show how ordinary bravery can evoke and transmit moral beauty.

chapters.title: Sensitivity to Goodness
chapters.description: The talk turns toward metta, Rumi, and the way devotion can make goodness feel ordinary, embodied, and shareable.

chapters.title: Moral Disgust
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts moral elevation with moral disgust and warns that ethical feeling can be pulled toward aversion and brutality.

chapters.title: Prophetic Speech
chapters.description: Using Frederick Douglass and Heschel, he considers moral language that burns past ordinary conscience without collapsing into moralizing.

chapters.title: Distance from Rumination
chapters.description: He brings in self-distancing research to distinguish helpful reflection from looping pain and to suggest a wider perspective on suffering.

chapters.title: Widening the Horizon
chapters.description: The talk expands into global care, mortality, and Kore-eda's After Life as a glimpse of being less trapped by becoming.

chapters.title: Already Broken
chapters.description: Matthew ties cessation of becoming to the broken tea cup teaching and shows how impermanence can open the heart to love and gratitude.

description: (Big Bear Retreat Center) These recordings are available publicly from a retreat held in person at Big Bear Retreat Center. Learn more about the center and upcoming offerings for retreats in nature, gathered in community. bigbearretreatcenter.org/upcoming-retreats/ Healing the Self, Loving the Self, Forgetting the Self Matthew Brensilver July 9 - 13, 2024 Co-sponsored by Insight Retreat Center (IRC) and Big Bear Retreat Center In this silent retreat, we explore how mindfulness supports the healing of the self. Many of the Buddhist teachings help us to tend to painful memory, old pains and the habits that compound suffering. As we become more gentle and loving towards experience, the self becomes less and less of a preoccupation. The more completely we accept ourselves, the easier it becomes to forget the self and rest in an awareness unencumbered by self-consciousness. This retreat includes sitting and walking meditation instructions, and dharma talks.

title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short teaching from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to unfold a quiet map of the path: generosity, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience. He moves from the felt meaning of sharing to the practical question of how to direct energy, make choices, and time action well. The talk stays grounded in ordinary examples and in the sense that practice is less about intensity than about steady, responsive care.
short_summary: A reflection on Ajahn Sucitto’s teaching that traces generosity and the rest of the path as expressions of interdependence, ethical care, and patient discernment.

chapters.title: Reading the quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Ajahn Sucitto passage and names the cluster of qualities it gathers together.

chapters.title: Generosity as interdependence
chapters.description: He expands generosity beyond material giving into an attitude shaped by relation, example, and shared blessing.

chapters.title: Sharing Dharma wisely
chapters.description: The talk turns to how advice lands, why example matters, and how generosity can be expressed without attachment to view.

chapters.title: Ethics and kindness
chapters.description: Matthew links interdependence with ethical choice, then pauses with a story and a study of random acts of kindness.

chapters.title: Morality and renunciation
chapters.description: He moves through self-respect, non-regret, and the question of enough, using personal anecdotes to show discernment in action.

chapters.title: Steady persistence
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts from peaks of effort to the daily steadiness that supports lasting change.

chapters.title: Patience and timing
chapters.description: Matthew closes with patience as sensitivity to ripeness, causality, and when a move is truly called for.

chapters.title: Closing reflections
chapters.description: He thanks Ajahn Sucitto, offers the teaching for consideration, and ends with a brief farewell.

title: Metta Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Afternoon Sit with Instructions
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver presents metta as something more than a pleasant mood: a capacity that makes insight practice bearable, especially when the heart meets the full intensity of suffering. He moves through the four brahmaviharas as four distinct kinds of love, then explains how metta can cultivate warmth, steady the mind, or bring buried grief and fear to the surface for healing. The talk closes with a simple practice of repeating phrases, listening for their effect, and widening the field of care to others without denying war, oppression, or loss.
short_summary: A grounded teaching on metta as the love that steadies insight, softens suffering, and widens naturally into compassion for all beings.

chapters.title: Love and Insight
chapters.description: Metta is introduced as essential to opening to the human condition without overwhelm, not as an optional extra to insight practice.

chapters.title: Four Species of Love
chapters.description: The talk reframes the brahmaviharas as metta, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity, each meeting a different condition of life.

chapters.title: Why Love Matters
chapters.description: Matthew explains why wisdom is incomplete without love and how metta can function as cultivation, concentration, or purification.

chapters.title: Meeting What Hurts
chapters.description: Metta is described as a practice that can draw grief, rage, and confusion into view and hold them with kindness rather than avoidance.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The instruction turns practical, emphasizing a sustainable posture, relaxation, and a quiet refusal to perform practice for anyone.

chapters.title: Remembering Goodness
chapters.description: Listeners are guided to bring to mind an image of goodness, sense the heart center, and let warmth soften clinging and self-harshness.

chapters.title: Abiding in Kindness
chapters.description: The teaching deepens into a felt sense that kindness makes it safe to let go and reminds the mind that aversion and fear cannot truly protect it.

chapters.title: Extending the Wish
chapters.description: The phrases widen from oneself to others, turning personal longing into a more universal wish for happiness, safety, and peace.

chapters.title: A Sublime Abiding
chapters.description: The close acknowledges suffering in the world while affirming that this species of love is not diminished by it and can be a noble dwelling of mind.

title: Guided Meditation: Listening to the Mind of Buddha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that gradually shifts from settling into the room toward a practice of listening: first to breath, body, sound, and the darkness behind closed eyes, then to a deeper receptivity he links to Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines. The talk emphasizes trusting awareness, letting silence beckon, and resting without over-managing experience. It closes by naming practice as a way to stop endlessly coping and simply be awake.
short_summary: A guided meditation on listening inwardly, trusting awareness, and resting beyond the compulsion to manage experience.

chapters.title: Arriving and Settling
chapters.description: A brief welcome leads into the invitation to sit and settle into stillness.

chapters.title: Silence Beckons
chapters.description: He names the silence and begins orienting attention toward the meditative field.

chapters.title: Simple Objects of Attention
chapters.description: The instruction turns to breath, body, sound, darkness behind the eyes, and possibly phrase or mantra as supports for attention.

chapters.title: Listening to Cohen
chapters.description: Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines become a doorway into the teaching about listening beyond the speaker and toward what does not need to be forced.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: He frames practice as listening to the mind of a Buddha, emphasizing receptivity and trust in awareness rather than effortful control.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and Rest
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into forgiving the mind, resting in silence, and meeting sorrow with a more spacious stance.

chapters.title: Patience and Urgency
chapters.description: He explores the balance between spiritual urgency and abiding patience as practice matures.

chapters.title: Beyond Coping
chapters.description: The close points to resting from endless coping, releasing the habit of sorting everything out, and finding wakefulness in simple being.

title: Ill-Will & Metta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how aversion narrows experience, and how metta opens a more honest and compassionate way to meet suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the problem
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces ill-will and frames it as a familiar way of suffering that the talk will examine closely.

chapters.title: The Dharma as a broad remedy
chapters.description: He uses pharmacology to argue that Dharma works through many mechanisms, not just the surface sign of a calm mind.

chapters.title: Purification and practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes concentration from purification and says the path includes peaks, valleys, and whatever is present now.

chapters.title: What and how
chapters.description: The talk turns to the 'what' and the deeper 'how' of practice, showing how motivations shape what we seem to know.

chapters.title: Craving shapes the view
chapters.description: He explores how craving and aversion thicken experience, using his own childhood sensitivity to show how ill-will can become a strategy for managing pain.

chapters.title: Mara and the turning inward
chapters.description: Brensilver describes the habitual impulses to approach, avoid, or ignore, then shows how clinging and aversion reinforce helplessness and suffering.

chapters.title: The heat of aversion
chapters.description: Using the boiling-pot simile, he explains how ill-will obscures judgment, while Agnes Callard, Ruth King, and the Dalai Lama help refine the view of anger as partially functional but never transformative.

chapters.title: Facing and owning anger
chapters.description: He turns to noticing ill-will directly, describing radical accountability as a training that stops externalizing blame without denying external causes.

chapters.title: Metta as antidote
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to metta as warm friendliness, free of possessiveness and control, and as a way of being moved by goodness and finding a home in it.

title: Guided Meditation - Grief and Compassion (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on grief that treats sorrow as a natural part of loving in an impermanent world. The practice moves from settling the body into phrases of tenderness, mercy, and reverence, with brief references to Leonard Cohen, anicca, and Stephen Levine to steady the listener in uncertainty. The tone stays intimate and practical, emphasizing that compassion is not an extra but a necessity when loss keeps arriving.
short_summary: A guided meditation on grief, impermanence, and compassion that invites the heart to meet sorrow with tenderness and mercy.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins by arriving and settling the body into the practice.

chapters.title: Welcoming the Body
chapters.description: Attention opens to bodily experience as a place to receive the practice gently.

chapters.title: Love and Grief
chapters.description: Brensilver names grief as woven into loving in a changing world and frames compassion as a necessity.

chapters.title: Impermanence and Tenderness
chapters.description: He invokes anicca to explain why change is startling and encourages mercy and love toward the heart.

chapters.title: Traveling in Fog
chapters.description: A Stephen Levine image of grief as traveling at night in fog supports patience with limited clarity.

chapters.title: Holding Sorrow
chapters.description: The practice shifts into explicit wishing and a steadier acceptance of what is here.

chapters.title: Love Deepened
chapters.description: He reflects that love without grief can be naive, and that loss can enrich love.

chapters.title: Tender Phrases
chapters.description: The meditation closes with repeated phrases of tenderness and reverence for the grieving world, ending in a quiet close.

title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.
short_summary: A brief reflection on belonging in Dharma, centered on the image of the path as a great shining world.

chapters.title: A shining world
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the poem’s image of the path as a great shining world rather than a fixed route.

chapters.title: Not fitting in
chapters.description: He examines the tendency to feel we must mold ourselves to match Dharma or Dharma culture.

chapters.title: Enter anywhere
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the freedom to begin practice from whatever conditions we actually have.

chapters.title: Belonging and home
chapters.description: He links belonging with a powerful human motivation and describes Dharma as something that can come to feel like home.

chapters.title: Nothing left behind
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that ultimately we are not forced to abandon any part of the heart in order to practice.

chapters.title: From every direction
chapters.description: He repeats the invitation to enter from anywhere, even when we feel awkward, remote, or contorted.

chapters.title: Affirming effort
chapters.description: The talk softens into encouragement to value one’s efforts without turning that recognition into self-absorption.

chapters.title: Rest and continue
chapters.description: He ends with a blessing to practice well, rest well, and keep going as new Dharma doors appear.

title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) William James said that death was the ‘worm at the core’ of the human condition that turns us all into ‘melancholy metaphysicians.’ A century later, awareness of mortality is documented to affect our thinking and emotional lives in powerful ways. It figures prominently in Buddhist practice. In what ways does consciousness of death distorts our view and lead us away from wisdom and compassion? Alternatively, how can we open to the truth of finitude such that our heart is softened? Can we intuit the freedom or love that might be released were we more deeply at peace with our mortality? In this evening program, we’ll consider the way death can harden or soften our heart – and how dharma practice might lead us to a life that feels complete. All are welcome.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on death not as a grim abstraction but as a way to clarify what matters, soften the heart, and loosen the pressure to build a story around the self. He moves through examples from literature, Buddhist teachings, psychology, and personal memory to show how mortality can either harden us into fear or open us toward grief, love, and responsibility. The talk ends by returning to practice as a kind of training in letting go and in making friendship with the mind.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on mortality, grief, and how accepting finitude can open compassion and make life feel more complete.

chapters.title: Opening the topic
chapters.description: He names death as the evening’s theme and uses a satirical Onion story to point to the acquisitive, grasping quality that can enter meditation.

chapters.title: Writing and dancing
chapters.description: A passage from Nicole Krauss becomes a way to contrast self-building and present-moment immediacy, recasting practice as more like dancing than narrating a life story.

chapters.title: Birth and mortality
chapters.description: He widens the frame to the rarity of being born at all, then introduces the five remembrances and Ajahn Lee’s praise of aging, illness, and death as treasures.

chapters.title: Practicing with death
chapters.description: He describes year-to-live practice, the social sensitivity of talking about mortality, hospice work, and the way one’s death shapes the lives of others.

chapters.title: Resisting and meaning
chapters.description: Stephen Jay Gould and Irvin Yalom help him balance acceptance with the reality that death can feel like an enemy and that death anxiety drives much human life.

chapters.title: Finitude and grief
chapters.description: Drawing on a book about secular faith, he argues that finitude makes responsibility possible and that dharma practice requires the courage to grieve, both personally and collectively.

chapters.title: Loss makes life vivid
chapters.description: Catherine Schultz and Walt Whitman are used to show that impermanence sharpens attention, makes ordinary life precious, and asks us to keep watch rather than keep control.

chapters.title: Fear, identity, and softness
chapters.description: He turns to Freud, Terror Management Theory, and Pema Chödrön to show how mortality can trigger defensive identity, or, when groundlessness is met well, awaken kindness.

chapters.title: Becoming and letting go
chapters.description: A Buddhist teaching on craving for becoming leads into an account of practice as surrendering the future, loosening self-clinging, and rehearsing the deathless by relinquishing control.

title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
short_summary: A reflective talk on busyness, selfing, and how loving acceptance can become a real ground for practice.

chapters.title: Ajahn Sucitto's quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the teaching and begins unpacking the claim that stopping is hard because self is always busy searching for ground.

chapters.title: Busyness as human condition
chapters.description: He broadens the theme into ordinary life, where even quiet time and retreat can feel organized around avoiding stillness.

chapters.title: Solitude and avoidance
chapters.description: Pascal and David Foster Wallace help frame the fear of being alone with the deeper pain that silence can expose.

chapters.title: Self, agitation, and comparison
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the subtle connection between selfing, defensiveness, and the habit of evaluating oneself as an object.

chapters.title: Mind and narrative self
chapters.description: He uses Nisargadatta and Dennett to suggest that what feels like a solid self is more like a narrative center folded out of experience.

chapters.title: Loving the mess
chapters.description: A long example from Heather Havrilesky and references to teachers like Ajahn Chah and Ajahn Sumedho support a gentler, less combative way of meeting our own messiness.

chapters.title: Non-dwelling and mortality
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward not dwelling in self at all, then toward death, Parfit, and the relief that comes when the self is less of a prison.

chapters.title: Closing blessing
chapters.description: Matthew ends with repeated invocation, a blessing for welfare, and a brief closing exchange with the group.

title: guided meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
